title: Coil-built square vessel
subtitle: A squared-off air-dry clay container built with coils
excerpt: A square-section vessel built from clay coils squared off at each course using a rib. The coil method gives the walls an organic texture on the outside while the interior is smoothed. Built in air-dry clay. Suitable as a pencil holder, small planter, or decorative object.
sourceNotes: Square-section coil pots appear across the coil-building tradition. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rope of clay rolled between the palms or on the board, used as the building unit in coil construction.
Scratching joining surfaces and brushing them with clay slurry before pressing together.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and join.

content.text: Building a square vessel with
coils

is a slightly more demanding exercise than a round coil pot because you must actively shape the walls at each course rather than letting them curve naturally. The rib is used to press each coil flat and perpendicular after stacking. The result has the slight softness of a hand-built form rather than the precision of a slab box, which is its character.

items.name: Air-dry clay
items.substitution: Grogged stoneware air-dry for larger versions

content.text: Steps
Press a flat square base approximately 80 mm x 80 mm and 8 mm thick.
Roll coils of even thickness and build up the walls in courses, joining each one firmly inside.
Smooth the interior joins with a metal rib; leave the exterior showing the coil texture.
Allow to reach leather-hard, then refine the top edge and any rough spots.
Dry slowly under loose plastic over two to three days to avoid cracking.
The base
Press a small amount of clay into a flat square approximately 80 mm x 80 mm and 8 mm thick. This can be cut from a rolled slab or formed by hand. Place it on the board.
Building the walls
1. First coil course
Score and slip

the perimeter of the base. Roll a coil 10 mm thick and press it around the outside edge of the base in a square path. Cut the coil where the ends meet and press them together. Smooth the inside seam down into the base. Use the straight edge of the metal rib or ruler to press the outside faces of the coil into flat planes, working along each of the four sides in turn. The corners will be slightly rounded but the faces will be flat.
2. Subsequent courses

Score the top of the previous coil, apply slurry, and press the next coil on top. Smooth the inside seam. Flatten the outside faces with the rib. Repeat until the vessel reaches 100 mm high (about seven or eight coils). Check periodically that the corners remain roughly aligned vertically; if they drift, press them back with the rib.
Reaching leather-hard and refining
Let the vessel stiffen to
leather-hard

under a plastic tent. At leather-hard, use the metal rib to scrape the outside walls flatter and sharpen the corners. The rib held at 90 degrees to the wall and drawn downward in a single stroke removes clay from high points and levels the surface. Work across each face, then refine the corners by pressing each adjacent face.
Drying and finishing

Continue drying under loose plastic for 24 hours, then open and allow to dry fully over two to three days. Sand any rough spots. Paint and seal with PVA varnish. As a pencil holder, the plain air-dry clay is sufficient; as a small planter, insert a plastic pot liner.
Variations
Make a rectangular vessel by building the coils in an oblong path rather than a square. Use a template cut from card to keep the footprint consistent.
Leave the outside walls showing the coil texture (smooth only the inside) for a tactile exterior surface.
Add a square lid using a rolled slab cut to 84 mm x 84 mm with a recessed knob.

items.cause: Coils were not pressed flat against a straight edge at each course
items.fix: Support each wall temporarily with a piece of card held flat against the outside while pressing subsequent coils

items.cause: Too much clay was removed when refining the corners at leather-hard
items.fix: Refine corners with light strokes. If a corner cracks, patch with slurry and fresh clay before fully dry
